one_line_takeaway: Across the strongest vulnerability-discovery benchmark, LLMs do not beat a tiny classifier over 23 hand-counted syntactic code metrics, and a causal analysis shows the LLMs are essentially relying on those same shallow statistics rather than genuine security understanding.
key_finding: On PrimeVul, the code-metrics classifier reaches F1 20.32 (+/-0.59) versus the best LLM UniXcoder's 20.69 (+/-1.43) using only 6% of the parameters (7M vs 125M); the difference is not statistically significant. A classifier trained on a single metric, the number of local variables (C11), already achieves over 90% of the full-metric F1. Combining metrics with LLM embeddings changes F1 by less than 0.14 percentage points, i.e. no complementary information. LLM embeddings linearly reconstruct metrics well (e.g. tree-node count T1: R2=0.91 for UniXcoder), and interventional patching shows a causal dependency (R2=0.42 UniXcoder, 0.38 PDBERT, 0.25 LineVul), meaning 20-40% of medium-LLM prediction variance is explained by shallow metrics. The two largest models (CodeGen 2.5, StarCoder 2) show R2 near 0 for causal dependence, which the authors attribute to missing metrics rather than deeper analysis (since their overall F1 is not higher). Conclusion: LLMs operate at a surface, statistical level equivalent to counting lines/loops.
core_contribution: A retrospective, critical evaluation showing that a classifier trained on only 23 syntactic code metrics performs on par with state-of-the-art LLMs for function-level vulnerability discovery, and a causal root-cause analysis (using Pearl's framework) demonstrating that LLM predictions rely on information equivalent to these shallow code metrics rather than deeper structural code understanding. The paper's own novel artifacts are: (1) a unified 'filter-map-reduce' family of syntactic code metrics (SCM) that generalizes historical complexity measures and recent security-oriented metrics into 23 metrics across four categories (code smells, complexity, memory, syntax tree); (2) the head-to-head comparison on PrimeVul; and (3) a four-precondition causal test (information access, no cross-information gain, prediction correlation, causal dependence). This is distinct from cited prior work (Chakraborty, Risse, Ding, Jimenez) that critiques data quality/labeling, and from GNN-limitation literature (over-squashing, over-smoothing) which the paper only cites.
threat_model: This is a security-domain paper but a methodological/critique paper, not a defense proposal, so it has no adversarial attacker threat model. The 'target' is the vulnerability-discovery task itself: detecting security-vulnerable C/C++ functions early in development. The implicit risk the paper addresses is not an attacker but methodological over-claiming: the field's belief that large LLMs meaningfully outperform simple baselines, which the paper shows is unsupported on the strongest current benchmark. No attack, exploit, or defense mechanism is proposed.
limitations: The authors flag three threats to validity: (1) Dataset dependence: findings are tied to PrimeVul; LLMs might win with much larger/more representative data, though PrimeVul is currently the largest, most refined benchmark. (2) Metric selection: the 23 syntactic metrics are not exhaustive (exclude dynamic/operational measures), so results are framed as a lower bound on the predictive power explainable via code metrics. (3) Hidden/confounding variables cannot be ruled out, so the causal estimate may under- or over-estimate metric influence. Additionally, absolute performance of all methods is very low (F1 ~20%, only ~4x above random's 4.42%), and the causal-dependence aggregation uses linear regression, likely underestimating a more complex true relationship.

datasets_used: PrimeVul (224,533 C/C++ functions from 755 open-source projects, 6,062 vulnerable; temporal train/test split; combines BigVul, CrossVul, CVEfixes, DiverseVul)
Stratified random subset of 2,000 samples from PrimeVul's test split (used for GPT-3.5/GPT-4o cost-limited evaluation)
quantitative_results: Parameter counts: SCM classifier ~7M (6.78e6), UniXcoder/PDBERT/LineVul 1.25e8, CodeGen 2.5 6.69e9, StarCoder 2 7.17e9, GPT-3.5 ~1e10, GPT-4o ~1e12; code metrics use 6% of UniXcoder's params (94% fewer)
Single metric C11 (number of local variables) achieves over 90% of the full-metric F1; 8 of 23 metrics individually reach >75% of combined performance
P1 information access, best-reconstructed metric T1 (tree nodes): R2=0.91 UniXcoder, 0.86 PDBERT, 0.80 CodeGen 2.5
P2 no cross-information gain: average F1 difference from adding metrics to LLM embeddings < 0.14 percentage points
P4 causal dependence R2: UniXcoder 0.42, PDBERT 0.38, LineVul 0.25; CodeGen 2.5 and StarCoder 2 approximately 0 (metrics explain 20-40% of medium-LLM prediction variance)
Alternative baselines: GNNs (Devign, ReVeal) max F1 17.8%+/-3.85; SAST tools (Rats, SemGrep) max F1 14.2%
